We are deeply heartbroken to share with our community and friends that Kiah Duggins was aboard American Airlines Flight 5342 from Wichita, Kansas, to Washington, D.C.

Kiah was a proud alumna of the Barton School of Business and Fairmount College of Liberal Arts and Sciences, graduating in the spring of 2017 with degrees in International Business, Economics, and Spanish. A recipient of the prestigious Clay Barton Scholarship in 2013, she joined a distinguished group of exceptionally talented students. During her time at Wichita State, Kiah co-founded the Shocker Food Locker, an initiative that continues to support students facing food insecurity. Her unwavering commitment to building more equitable communities is a legacy we can all carry forward within Shocker Nation.

Born and raised in Wichita, Kiah was the daughter of Dr. Maurice and Gwen Duggins, who instilled in her a deep commitment to social justice through their own dedication to serving underserved communities in education and healthcare. After graduating from Wichita State, she went on to earn her J.D. from Harvard Law School, where she served as President of the Harvard Legal Aid Bureau. It was no surprise that after law school, Kiah became an attorney with the Civil Rights Corps in Washington, D.C., continuing her family’s legacy of advocacy and service.

During her tenure as an attorney, Kiah litigated on behalf of movements challenging unconstitutional policing and money bail practices. She studied prison industrial complex abolition and movement lawyering as a Law 4 Black Lives Fellow and integrated those concepts into her daily work. 

Her family remains active in the Wichita community, and this tragedy has profoundly impacted all who knew her. Today, we mourn the loss of one of our own. To those who had the privilege of knowing Kiah, our hearts are with you during this incredibly difficult time.

Kiah was a beacon of light, a testament to perseverance, and an inspiration to all who dream big and work tirelessly to achieve their goals. We hope her legacy continues to inspire our students, serving as a reminder of what is possible through determination, passion, and purpose.

Big news for research at Wichita State!

With a generous $1 million lead gift from Lynn and Sherry Nichols, we’re excited to launch efforts to build the new, state-of-the-art Shocker Fly Lab — an enclosed flight space designed for UAV research, design and testing. 

The enclosed flight space will position WSU at the forefront of unmanned aerial system education and innovation. The space will bring hands-on learning for students, industry-sponsored competitions and groundbreaking faculty research.

Who’s that building?

It’s one of the most iconic sights on campus — the clock tower that watches over Wichita State. Behind the bricks and bell tower, Morrison Hall has a story worth telling.

This landmark started as a library. Twice. The first was built in 1909. The university’s first president, Nathan J. Morrison — for whom the building was later named — died just one day after the groundbreaking.

By the 1930s, the university had outgrown its original library, and a new library opened in 1939 — the building we now know as Morrison Hall. When Ablah Library was completed in 1962, the building was renamed and repurposed.

Today, Morrison Hall houses the Office of the President, the campus post office, Shocker Printing Solutions, Academic Affairs and other administrative offices. Renovations are underway to preserve and modernize this campus centerpiece for generations to come.

In 1981, Wichita State's Women’s Varsity Four stunned the rowing world by winning a national title at the prestigious Dad Vail Regatta — just six years after the team was founded. Competing against powerhouse programs, they proved grit, rhythm and relentless early mornings on the river could take them all the way to gold.

This month, we celebrate 50 years of @shockerrowing — and the legacy of teamwork, perseverance and underdog victories that continue to define the program.

Join us April 26–27 for a weekend honoring five decades on the river. Details at 🔗 link in bio.

Who's that building?

🥎 Wilkins Stadium has been home to Wichita State Softball since 1998, but its story began years earlier on a patch of uneven grass east of Henrion Hall. It wasn’t much, but it was the starting point for something bigger. In 1997, construction began on a new, modern facility, giving the team a home worthy of its growing success.

Now it’s time for the next chapter — a master plan to transform Wilkins Stadium into a championship-ready complex. We can't wait to cheer on our Shockers in the new stadium!

WSU Physical Therapy student stepped into the challenges many older adults face with a homemade aging-simulation suit. 

“It’s hot, it’s tiring, and it makes the simplest tasks frustrating,” said Dr. Justin Smith, assistant professor and board-certified geriatric clinical specialist. “But if even one student walks away with a deeper understanding of what their future patients live with, it’s worth it.”

Daniel Reichart uses advanced simulations to study how a CubeSat detector can identify space particles like electrons, protons and alpha particles. His work, guided by physics professor Dr. Nick Solomey and supported by NASA’s Jumpstart Program, contributes to understanding radiation hazards in space and showcases how undergraduates can play a vital role in space research. 🔗 Full story in bio.

WSU senior Jeffrey Blessant took his first-ever trip abroad this January, exploring the heart of Italy with support from the Garvey family.

Surrounded by gelato shops, cafés and historic beauty, he found something unexpected — the realization that even in a culture so different, there’s a shared human connection that makes the world feel smaller.

As a psychology and creative writing major, this experience gave him a new lens to view human behavior, storytelling and the ways people across cultures find meaning in everyday life.
